question: If two studies' results yield $\mathrm{P}<0.001$ and $\mathrm{P}=0.02$, why is it not necessarily true, as noted in section 16.3.6, that the former has found a stronger effect than the latter?
answer: The P value is a measure of the strength of evidence against the null hypothesis. It does not indicate the magnitude of the observed effect. Two clinical trials of different sizes may yield the same treatment effect but different P values. For continuous variables the P value also depends upon the variability (see exercise 9.6).
